context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out this reflection on neoclassical vs dependency theories of development. I’m a little lost on how to answer whether planning is necessary in each model, and how to connect dependency theory to current critiques of the WTO and “policy space.”
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the key tensions in the prompt. Let's start at the beginning. In neoclassical economics, what is the basic assumption about how markets work when left alone? What does that suggest about whether a planner — or the state — needs to step in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable overview of their confusion. Rather than scaffolding all parts at once, I should start with the most foundational concept — what neoclassical economics actually says about markets and state intervention — and let the student build from there. This keeps answers bite-sized and uses guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I think the basic idea is that markets are supposed to allocate resources efficiently if they’re left alone, like through prices and competition. So I’m guessing that means planning isn’t seen as very necessary, except maybe if something goes wrong? I’m not fully sure what counts as “goes wrong” here.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. That instinct about 'something going wrong' is actually a key concept in economics — can you think of any specific situations where a market might fail to allocate resources efficiently? Even everyday examples work.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly grasped the core neoclassical assumption and is already intuitively sensing the concept of market failures without naming it. I should affirm the direction and push them to sharpen that intuition by naming and defining what 'goes wrong' means in neoclassical terms. This keeps the discovery process going without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: Maybe monopolies? And pollution too, because the cost isn’t fully paid by the company making it. I’m blanking on the formal category names, but I think the point is that markets don’t always handle those cases well.
exchanges.tutor: Those are exactly the right examples — pollution is what economists call an 'externality,' and monopolies relate to imperfect competition. These fall under the broader category of 'market failures.' So now, given that neoclassical economists do acknowledge these cases, how would you characterize their overall attitude toward planning or state intervention — is it welcomed, tolerated, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified two classic market failures (monopolies/imperfect competition and externalities) without knowing the formal terms. I should affirm this and nudge them to name the concepts, then connect this back to the assignment question about planning's role in the neoclassical model.

exchanges.student: I’d say it’s tolerated more than welcomed. Like, intervention seems justified only in limited cases where the market fails, not as the main driver of development. So planning feels kind of corrective in that model, not central.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really good way to put it — 'corrective, not central.' Now, dependency theory comes from a very different place. If neoclassical theory focuses on internal market dynamics, what do you think dependency theorists emphasize instead as the main obstacle to development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a genuinely good insight — planning as 'corrective' rather than central in the neoclassical model. This is a strong foundation. Now I should pivot them toward dependency theory, which is the next major component of the prompt, using a question that connects back to what they've just established.

exchanges.student: I think they focus more on the international system, like poorer countries being stuck in unequal relationships with richer ones. Maybe the problem isn’t just bad internal markets but the whole structure of trade and power. Is that basically the core idea?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the core idea. So now think about this: if the problem is that external structure trapping poorer countries, does that make dependency theorists *against* planning, or might they actually see a strong role for it? What would they need planning *to do*, given their diagnosis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the structural and international dimension of dependency theory. Now I should push them to think about what that implies for planning — the prompt specifically asks whether dependency theorists are hostile to planning, which is a nuanced question worth exploring through their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: Then I don’t think they’d be against planning at all — actually probably the opposite. I’m guessing they’d want planning to protect domestic industry, reduce dependence on foreign capital or imports, and give the state a bigger role in changing the economy. I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic though.
exchanges.tutor: That's not simplistic at all — you've essentially described what became known as import substitution industrialization, a real policy program inspired by these ideas. Now, the last part of the prompt asks about echoes of dependency theory in current critiques of institutions like the WTO and arguments for 'policy space.' What do you think 'policy space' even means in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned that dependency theorists would favor active state planning as a tool of structural transformation, and even identified specific mechanisms like import substitution and reducing foreign dependence. This is not simplistic at all — it's quite accurate. I should affirm this and now bridge toward the final part of the prompt, which connects dependency thinking to current WTO critiques and 'policy space.'

exchanges.student: I think “policy space” means a country having room to make its own economic decisions without being boxed in by global rules. So like, if WTO rules or loan conditions stop a state from using tariffs, subsidies, or industrial policy, then its policy space is smaller. That part actually clicks a bit more now.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that clicks for a reason — you've basically already built the argument. So what's the echo of dependency theory here? Why would a dependency theorist be critical of institutions like the WTO constraining a developing country's policy space?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly grasped the meaning of policy space. Now I should push them to make the explicit connection between this concept and dependency theory — that's the key analytical move the prompt is asking for. This is the last major conceptual piece before they should start writing.

exchanges.student: I think the echo is that both see global rules as not neutral. A dependency theorist would probably say institutions like the WTO lock poorer countries into a system shaped by richer countries, which makes it harder for them to use planning or industrial policy to change their position. I’m still not sure how strongly I should say it though, since I don’t want to overstate that current critiques are exactly the same as old dependency theory.
exchanges.tutor: That caution is actually a sign of strong thinking — the prompt says 'echoes,' not 'identical arguments,' which already gives you room for that nuance. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. What would you say is the through-line connecting all of them? Try putting it in one or two sentences — that could help you find your opening argument for the piece.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made the key analytical connection and is now showing intellectual maturity by questioning how strongly to make the claim. This is excellent critical thinking. I should affirm this nuance and encourage them to think about how to frame that distinction in their writing — without writing it for them. We've now covered all four parts of the prompt, so I should nudge them toward actually drafting.

exchanges.student: I think the through-line is that different development theories disagree on whether underdevelopment comes mainly from internal market problems or from unequal global structures, and that changes how they view planning. Neoclassical economists mostly treat planning as limited and corrective, while dependency theorists see it as a necessary tool for reshaping a country’s position in the global economy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid through-line — it captures the key tension well. You have everything you need to start writing. One thing to keep in mind as you draft: the prompt has four distinct questions, so make sure each one gets addressed. What part of the piece are you planning to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and coherent through-line that captures the core tension of the prompt. This is a strong foundation for their piece. We've had about 8 exchanges and covered all the conceptual ground. I should affirm this and push them to start drafting, since the next step is getting words on the page — not more conceptual discussion.

exchanges.student: I’ll probably start with the neoclassical part, since that feels clearer now, and then use the contrast to move into dependency theory. I’m still a little worried about making the transition to the WTO/policy space section feel natural.
exchanges.tutor: You already made that transition naturally in our conversation — think back to how you connected the two. What was the logical step you took that linked dependency theory's critique of global structures to the WTO argument? That connection is your bridge. Now go start drafting and come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear writing plan and is showing good structural thinking. The concern about the transition is a writing craft question, which I should not answer for them. Instead, I should nudge them to think about what logical bridge connects dependency theory to the WTO critique — they already have the pieces, they just need to trust their own reasoning. It's time to encourage them to start drafting.
